title: Learning quantum disentanglement scheduling from reduced states via modular hybrid policies
authors: Y. -X. Xiao, J. -Z. Han, Z. Zheng, Z. -H. Zhang, M. Xue, J. Li, X. Lv
abstract: Quantum control with restricted state access is central to near-term quantum devices, where full wave-function information is unavailable. We study this problem through multiqubit disentanglement scheduling from partial observations, where a controller receives only two-qubit reduced density matrices and selects which qubit pair to disentangle at each step. We introduce a modular hybrid quantum--classical policy framework consisting of classical preprocessing, a parameterized quantum circuit as a compact nonlinear latent block, and classical postprocessing for pair-selection probabilities. Benchmarking 4-, 5-, and 6-qubit tasks, we find that preprocessing is the dominant factor governing performance under reduced-state observations, while the quantum module provides a conditional compact representation whose utility depends on the input features and model budget. We further identify a performance--efficiency trade-off across policy families and find that increasing circuit width is generally more useful than increasing depth. These results provide practical design principles for hybrid policies in reduced-information quantum control.
summary: The paper proposes a hybrid quantum-classical machine learning approach to control the disentanglement of multiple qubits using only partial information from reduced density matrices. It demonstrates that while quantum circuits provide useful compact representations, the success of the control policy depends heavily on classical preprocessing. The findings offer design principles for optimizing hybrid controllers in resource-constrained quantum environments.
main_problem: Developing an efficient quantum control strategy for multiqubit disentanglement when only partial information (two-qubit reduced density matrices) is available.
main_result: The study identifies that classical preprocessing is the primary driver of performance, while the quantum module acts as a compact latent representation; it also finds that increasing circuit width is more effective than increasing depth for policy efficiency.
method: A modular hybrid quantum-classical policy framework comprising classical preprocessing, a parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) acting as a nonlinear latent block, and classical postprocessing for pair-selection probabilities.
model_or_system: A multiqubit system (benchmarked on 4, 5, and 6-qubit tasks) undergoing a disentanglement scheduling process based on observations of two-qubit reduced density matrices.
key_observables: Two-qubit reduced density matrices and pair-selection probabilities.
important_parameters: Circuit width, circuit depth, and model budget.
main_assumptions: The controller has restricted access to the state, receiving only partial information via reduced density matrices rather than the full wave-function.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of partial-observation control, describes the modular hybrid policy architecture, presents benchmarking results across different qubit numbers, and analyzes the performance-efficiency trade-offs and architectural design principles.
why_interesting: This is relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it addresses the practical challenge of controlling complex quantum states using only local, partial measurements, which is a realistic scenario for near-term hardware.
